skill.name: webapp-testing
skill.description: Playwright-based toolkit for testing local web applications, inspecting rendered pages, capturing screenshots, and collecting browser console logs.
skill.summary: Test and debug local web applications with Playwright automation.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns. Most weak-crypto and hardcoded URL findings are false positives from license text or localhost examples, but the helper script intentionally runs user-supplied server commands with shell=True. This appears legitimate for local webapp testing, so publication is acceptable with a clear command-execution warning.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Server Commands Run Through Shell
medium_findings.description: The helper accepts --server values and starts them with subprocess.Popen using shell=True. This is useful for commands such as cd and npm run dev, but any untrusted server string could execute arbitrary shell syntax.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell=True call is confirmed and its input comes from command-line arguments. The context is a local testing helper, so this is a real but expected command-execution risk rather than evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Follow-Up Command Execution
medium_findings.description: After servers are ready, the helper runs the remaining command arguments with subprocess.run. This behavior is explicit and needed for automation, but users should run only commands they trust.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is direct and confirmed, but subprocess.run receives an argument list without shell=True. Risk depends on what the user chooses to execute.

low_findings.title: Localhost Network Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to localhost web applications or Apache license pages. No evidence found of data exfiltration or calls to suspicious external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited application URLs use localhost, and the external URLs are standard Apache license references. This strongly supports a false-positive network finding.

low_findings.title: Expected Screenshot and Log File Writes
low_findings.description: Example scripts write screenshots and console logs to output or temporary paths. These writes are expected artifacts for browser testing and do not target sensitive files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are used for logs and screenshots generated by browser automation. There is no evidence of sensitive file reads, persistence, or hidden writes.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto Matches Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns in markdown and license text. No cryptographic API use or hashing implementation was found at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are natural-language documentation or license prose, not code. This makes the weak-crypto alerts very likely false positives.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Examples in Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill documentation shows users how to run local test servers and Playwright scripts. These are operational examples, but users should avoid copying commands from untrusted projects.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly intends local development automation. It still encourages command execution, so the residual risk is real but lower than hidden execution in code.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen With shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper launches server commands through a shell. Treat every --server value as trusted local input only.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly sets shell=True on a command taken from parsed arguments. This is a known command-injection hazard if untrusted input reaches it.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Automation Writes Local Artifacts
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples write screenshots and console logs to local paths during testing. This is expected, but it should be run in a workspace-safe context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The writes are visible and tied to testing artifacts. The concern is operational data exposure, not malicious file modification.

content.user_title: Test Local Web Apps with Playwright
content.value_statement: Manual browser checks miss rendered-state bugs and console errors. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to automate local webapp testing with Playwright.
actual_capabilities: Guides agents to write Python Playwright scripts for local web applications.
Provides a helper for starting one or more local servers before automation runs.
Shows how to wait for dynamic pages before inspecting the rendered DOM.
Demonstrates screenshot capture for visual inspection and debugging.
Demonstrates browser console log capture during automated interactions.
Includes examples for static HTML files, element discovery, and UI actions.
limitations: Requires Playwright and browser dependencies to be installed in the environment.
Designed for local applications, not production monitoring or remote security scanning.
Server commands are executed locally and must come from trusted project sources.
Examples are templates and often need selector or port changes for each application.

use_cases.title: Verify a Local Frontend Change
use_cases.description: Run a local app, open it in Playwright, interact with key controls, and capture screenshots for review.

use_cases.title: Debug Rendered UI State
use_cases.description: Wait for dynamic pages to finish loading, inspect visible elements, and identify reliable selectors.

use_cases.title: Collect Console Evidence
use_cases.description: Capture browser console messages during a workflow to diagnose JavaScript errors and warnings.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Local App
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a running development server.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the webapp-testing skill to open my local app on port 5173. Wait for the page to finish loading, list the visible buttons and links, and take a screenshot.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Basic User Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Validate a simple UI path with Playwright.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the webapp-testing skill to write and run a Playwright script for my local app. Start the server if needed, click through the main workflow, and report visible failures.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Console Errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the webapp-testing skill to capture console logs while exercising the dashboard flow. Summarize errors, warnings, and the action that triggered each message.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Multi-Service Testing
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for apps with backend and frontend servers.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the webapp-testing skill with the server helper to start my backend and frontend, wait for both ports, run a focused Playwright regression script, and clean up all processes afterward.

output_examples.input: Inspect the app running on localhost and find clickable elements.
output: The page loaded after the network became idle.
Visible controls included the navigation links, primary action button, and search input.
A screenshot was saved for visual comparison.

output_examples.input: Capture console messages during the dashboard workflow.
output: The workflow completed through the requested dashboard action.
Two warnings and one error appeared in the browser console.
The error was tied to the chart rendering step.

output_examples.input: Run a local app with its server command and test the signup form.
output: The server started on the expected port and was stopped after the run.
The signup form accepted input and submitted successfully.
Final screenshots were captured before and after submission.

best_practices: Run helper scripts with --help before using them in a project.
Use trusted local server commands and review package scripts before execution.
Wait for network idle or stable selectors before inspecting dynamic pages.
anti_patterns: Do not run server commands copied from untrusted repositories without review.
Do not inspect dynamic pages before JavaScript has finished rendering important content.
Do not rely on brittle selectors when accessible roles or stable IDs are available.

faq.question: Does this skill test production websites?
faq.answer: It is intended for local web applications and static HTML files. Production testing requires separate access controls and monitoring practices.

faq.question: Which browser tool does it use?
faq.answer: The examples use Python Playwright with Chromium in headless mode.

faq.question: Can it start my development server?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper script can start one or more local server commands and wait for their ports.

faq.question: Is command execution involved?
faq.answer: Yes. The server helper executes local commands supplied by the user, so commands must be reviewed and trusted.

faq.question: Can it capture screenshots?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show full-page screenshots for rendered pages and static HTML files.

faq.question: Can it inspect console logs?
faq.answer: Yes. The console logging example records browser console messages during page interaction.
